vignette: A 4-month-old infant presents in December with nasal congestion, cough, and wheezing for 3 days. He is tachypneic (RR 60) with subcostal retractions. SpO2 is 89% on room air. RSV nasal swab is positive. What is the BEST management per AAP 2024 guidelines?

explanation.whyCorrect: AAP 2024: Bronchiolitis management is SUPPORTIVE. Oxygen for SpO2 <90%, nasal suctioning, hydration. NO role for albuterol, steroids, or antibiotics.
explanation.clinicalPearl: Bronchiolitis = Supportive care ONLY. No albuterol, no steroids.

vignette: A 3-week-old neonate presents with rectal temperature 38.3°C. She is feeding poorly and is lethargic. No focal source on exam. What is the MOST appropriate management?

options.text: CBC, blood culture, discharge on amoxicillin
Full septic workup (blood, urine, CSF) + IV ampicillin/gentamicin + Admit
Urine culture only, follow up in 24h
CBC and CRP, if normal discharge

explanation.whyCorrect: Neonate <28 days with fever: Full septic workup + IV antibiotics + Hospital admission.

whyWrong.E: Normal CBC/CRP does NOT rule out SBI.

explanation.clinicalPearl: Febrile neonate <28 days = Full septic workup + IV antibiotics + Admit.

vignette: A 7-year-old with mild persistent asthma uses albuterol 4-5 times per week. She is on no controller medication. What is the BEST next step per GINA 2024?

explanation.whyCorrect: GINA 2024: NO MORE SABA ALONE. Step 1-2 = Low-dose ICS-formoterol AS NEEDED.
explanation.clinicalPearl: GINA 2024: All patients should receive ICS-containing therapy.

vignette: A 3-year-old Asian male presents with 6 days of fever >39°C, bilateral conjunctival injection, strawberry tongue, and a polymorphous rash. What is the BEST treatment?

explanation.whyCorrect: Kawasaki disease: IVIG 2g/kg + High-dose aspirin within 10 days. Reduces coronary aneurysm risk from 25% to <5%.
explanation.clinicalPearl: Kawasaki: IVIG + Aspirin within 10 days of fever onset.

vignette: A 2-year-old presents with barking cough, stridor at rest, and retractions. SpO2 92%. What is the BEST initial treatment?

explanation.clinicalPearl: Croup: Dexamethasone for all + Epinephrine for moderate-severe.

vignette: A 4-day-old term breastfed infant with 8% weight loss has total bilirubin 18 mg/dL. He is lethargic. What is the BEST management?

explanation.whyCorrect: Above phototherapy threshold: Intensive phototherapy + Optimize feeding.
explanation.clinicalPearl: Use AAP phototherapy nomogram based on GA, risk factors, and hour-specific bilirubin.

vignette: A 18-month-old male has a 3-minute generalized seizure with fever 39°C. He is now at baseline with normal exam. What is the BEST management?

explanation.whyCorrect: Simple febrile seizure: Reassurance, antipyretics, parent education. No AEDs, no EEG.
explanation.clinicalPearl: Simple febrile seizure: Reassurance only. 30% recurrence, excellent prognosis.

vignette: A 6-month-old febrile female has urine culture E. coli >100K CFU/mL. She appears well but febrile for 3 days. What is recommended?

options.text: IV ceftriaxone until afebrile then oral antibiotics + Renal US

explanation.whyCorrect: Febrile infant with UTI: IV antibiotics + Renal US.

whyWrong.A: Oral alone insufficient for febrile infant.

explanation.clinicalPearl: Febrile UTI in infants: IV antibiotics + Renal US.

vignette: A healthy 2-month-old infant received Hep B at birth. Which vaccines are recommended per CDC 2024?

options.text: DTaP, Hib, IPV, PCV13, Rotavirus, Hep B #2

explanation.whyCorrect: CDC 2024: 2-month = DTaP, Hib, IPV, PCV13, Rotavirus, Hep B #2.
explanation.clinicalPearl: 2-month vaccines: 6 shots. First major immunization visit.

vignette: A 20-month-old male has poor eye contact, no pointing/waving, no words, and stopped babbling at 15 months. What is the BEST next step?

options.text: M-CHAT-R/F screening + Refer for ASD evaluation + Audiology

explanation.whyCorrect: ASD red flags: M-CHAT-R/F + Referral + Audiology. Early intervention critical.
explanation.clinicalPearl: M-CHAT at 18 & 24 months. Early intervention = Better outcomes.
